params.name: FX
params.description: an arbitrary effect (N inputs and N outputs) to apply to the input bus

symbols.name: mixLinearClamp
symbols.summary: Linear mixer for `N` buses, each with `C` channels. The output will be a sum of 2 buses determined by the mixing index `mix`. 0 produces the first bus, 1 produces the second, and so on. `mix` is clamped automatically. For example, `mixLinearClamp(4, 1, 1)` will weight its 4 inputs by `(0, 1, 0, 0)`. Similarly, `mixLinearClamp(4, 1, 1.1)` will weight its 4 inputs by `(0,.9,.1,0)`.

params.name: N
params.description: the number of input buses

params.name: C
params.description: the number of channels in each bus

params.name: mix
params.description: the mixing index, continuous in [0;N-1].

symbols.testCode:
ef = library("misceffects.lib");
mixLinearClamp_test = (1,0,0,0) : ef.mixLinearClamp(4, 1, 1.2);

symbols.name: mixLinearLoop
symbols.summary: Linear mixer for `N` buses, each with `C` channels. Refer to `mixLinearClamp`. `mix` will loop for multiples of `N`. For example, `mixLinearLoop(4, 1, 0)` has the same effect as `mixLinearLoop(4, 1, -4)` and `mixLinearLoop(4, 1, 4)`.

params.name: N
params.description: the number of input buses

params.name: C
params.description: the number of channels in each bus

params.name: mix
params.description: the mixing index (N-1) selects the last bus, and 0 or N selects the 0th bus.

symbols.testCode:
ef = library("misceffects.lib");
mixLinearLoop_test = (1,0,0,0) : ef.mixLinearLoop(4, 1, -0.3);

symbols.name: mixPowerClamp
symbols.summary: Constant-power mixer for `N` buses, each with `C` channels. The output will be a sum of 2 buses determined by the mixing index `mix`. 0 produces the first bus, 1 produces the second, and so on. `mix` is clamped automatically. `mixPowerClamp(4, 1, 1)` will weight its 4 inputs by `(0, 1./sqrt(2), 0, 0)`. Similarly, `mixPowerClamp(4, 1, 1.5)` will weight its 4 inputs by `(0,.5,.5,0)`.

params.name: N
params.description: the number of input buses

params.name: C
params.description: the number of channels in each bus

params.name: mix
params.description: the mixing index, continuous in [0;N-1].

symbols.testCode:
ef = library("misceffects.lib");
mixPowerClamp_test = (1,0,0,0) : ef.mixPowerClamp(4, 1, 1.5);

symbols.name: mixPowerLoop
symbols.summary: Constant-power mixer for `N` buses, each with `C` channels. Refer to `mixPowerClamp`. `mix` will loop for multiples of `N`. For example, `mixPowerLoop(4, 1, 0)` has the same effect as `mixPowerLoop(4, 1, -4)` and `mixPowerLoop(4, 1, 4)`.

params.name: N
params.description: the number of input buses

params.name: C
params.description: the number of channels in each bus

params.name: mix
params.description: the mixing index (N-1) selects the last bus, and 0 or N selects the 0th bus.

symbols.testCode:
ef = library("misceffects.lib");
mixPowerLoop_test = (1,0,0,0) : ef.mixPowerLoop(4, 1, -0.5);

symbols.name: tapeStop
symbols.summary: A tape-stop effect, like putting a finger on a vinyl record player.

params.name: C
params.description: The number of input and output channels.

params.name: LAGRANGE_ORDER
params.description: The order of the Lagrange interpolation on the delay line. [2-3] recommended.

params.name: MAX_TIME_SAMP
params.description: Maximum stop time in samples

params.name: crossfade
params.description: A crossfade in samples to apply when resuming normal playback. Crossfade is not applied during the enabling of the tape-stop.

params.name: gainAlpha
params.description: During the tape-stop, lower alpha stays louder longer. Safe values are in the range [.01,2].

params.name: stopAlpha
params.description: `stopAlpha==1` represents a linear deceleration (constant force). `stopAlpha<1` represents an initially weaker, then stronger force. `stopAlpha>1` represents an initially stronger, then weaker force. Safe values are in the range [.01,2].

params.name: stopTime
params.description: Desired duration of the stop time, in samples.

params.name: stop
params.description: When `stop` becomes positive, the tape-stop effect will start. When `stop` becomes zero, normal audio will resume via crossfade.

params.name: ratio

symbols.summary: An environment with sine/cosine oscsillators with exponentially decaying harmonics based on direct summation formula.

params.name: f0
params.description: base frequency

symbols.name: twin_osc
symbols.summary: Twin oscillator using a time-varying comb filter for PWM, morph, or detune. Sawtooth through feedforward comb filter: `y = x - g*x[n-d]`. Based on: "Virtual Analog Synthesis with a Time-Varying Comb Filter" D. Lowenfels, AES Convention 115, October 2003. Modes (comb filter configurations): * 0 = PWM: `y = x - x[d]` - pulse width modulation * 1 = MORPH: `y = x - amt*x[d]` - saw to square morphing * 2 = DETUNE: `y = x + x[d]` - summing comb

params.name: freq
params.description: frequency in Hz

params.name: amt
params.description: effect amount (0-1), controls comb delay time

params.name: detune
params.description: additional delay offset in samples

params.name: mode
params.description: mode selector (0=PWM, 1=MORPH, 2=DETUNE)

symbols.testCode:
os = library("oscillators.lib");
twin_osc_test = os.twin_osc(220, 0.5, 0, 0);

symbols.name: rpm
symbols.summary: An environment with RPM (Recursive Phase Modulation) oscillators. Based on Norio Tomisawa's 1981 Yamaha patent for feedback FM synthesis. Uses a "hunting filter" (2-point moving average) in the feedback path to suppress Nyquist-rate limit cycle oscillations at high modulation indices.

params.name: freq
params.description: frequency in Hz

params.name: beta
params.description: modulation depth (0 to ~1.5)

symbols.name: SR
symbols.summary: Current sampling rate (between 1 and 192000Hz). Constant during program execution. Setting this value to a constant will allow the compiler to optimize the code by computing constant expressions at compile time, and can be valuable for performance, especially on embedded systems. -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

symbols.name: BS
symbols.summary: Current block-size (between 1 and 16384 frames). Can change during the execution. -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

symbols.name: tablesize
symbols.summary: Oscillator table size. This value is used to define the size of the table used by the oscillators. It is usually a power of 2 and can be lowered to save memory. The default value is 65536. -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

basicBlock_test = 0,0,0 : pm.basicBlock;

symbols.name: chain
symbols.summary: Creates a chain of bidirectional blocks. Blocks must have 3 inputs and outputs. The first input/output carry left going waves, the second input/output carry right going waves, and the third input/output is used to carry any potential output signal to the end of the algorithm. The implied one sample delay created by the `~` operator is generalized to the left and right going waves. Thus, `n` blocks in `chain()` will add an `n` samples delay to both left and right going waves.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
chain_test = 0,0,0 : pm.chain(pm.in(0.1) : pm.basicBlock);

symbols.name: inLeftWave
symbols.summary: Adds a signal to left going waves anywhere in a [`chain`](#chain) of blocks.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
inLeftWave_test = 0,0,0 : pm.inLeftWave(0.25);

symbols.name: inRightWave
symbols.summary: Adds a signal to right going waves anywhere in a [`chain`](#chain) of blocks.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
inRightWave_test = 0,0,0 : pm.inRightWave(0.25);

symbols.name: in
symbols.summary: Adds a signal to left and right going waves anywhere in a [`chain`](#chain) of blocks.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
in_test = 0,0,0 : pm.in(0.25);

symbols.name: outLeftWave
symbols.summary: Sends the signal of left going waves to the output channel of the [`chain`](#chain).
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
outLeftWave_test = pm.outLeftWave(0.1, 0.2, 0.3);

symbols.name: outRightWave
symbols.summary: Sends the signal of right going waves to the output channel of the [`chain`](#chain).
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
outRightWave_test = pm.outRightWave(0.1, 0.2, 0.3);

symbols.name: out
symbols.summary: Sends the signal of right and left going waves to the output channel of the [`chain`](#chain).
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
out_test = pm.out(0.1, 0.2, 0.3);

symbols.name: terminations
symbols.summary: Creates terminations on both sides of a [`chain`](#chain) without closing the inputs and outputs of the bidirectional signals chain. As for [`chain`](#chain), this function adds a 1 sample delay to the bidirectional signal, both ways. Of course, this function can be nested within a [`chain`](#chain).
symbols.usage: b = chain(D : E : F); // bidirectional chain of blocks (D, E, F, etc.)

io.raw: b = chain(D : E : F); // bidirectional chain of blocks (D, E, F, etc.)

symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
terminations_test = 0,0,0 : pm.terminations(*(-1), pm.basicBlock, *(-1));

symbols.name: lTermination
symbols.summary: Creates a termination on the left side of a [`chain`](#chain) without closing the inputs and outputs of the bidirectional signals chain. This function adds a 1 sample delay near the termination and can be nested within another [`chain`](#chain).
symbols.usage: b = chain(D : E : F); // bidirectional chain of blocks (D, E, F, etc.)

io.raw: b = chain(D : E : F); // bidirectional chain of blocks (D, E, F, etc.)

symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
lTermination_test = 0,0,0 : pm.lTermination(*(-1), pm.basicBlock);

symbols.name: rTermination
symbols.summary: Creates a termination on the right side of a [`chain`](#chain) without closing the inputs and outputs of the bidirectional signals chain. This function adds a 1 sample delay near the termination and can be nested within another [`chain`](#chain).
symbols.usage: b = chain(D : E : F); // bidirectional chain of blocks (D, E, F, etc.)

io.raw: b = chain(D : E : F); // bidirectional chain of blocks (D, E, F, etc.)

symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
rTermination_test = 0,0,0 : pm.rTermination(pm.basicBlock, *(-1));

symbols.name: closeIns
symbols.summary: Closes the inputs of a bidirectional chain in all directions.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
closeIns_test = pm.closeIns;

symbols.name: closeOuts
symbols.summary: Closes the outputs of a bidirectional chain in all directions except for the main signal output (3d output).
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
closeOuts_test = 0,0,0 : pm.closeOuts;

symbols.name: endChain
symbols.summary: Closes the inputs and outputs of a bidirectional chain in all directions except for the main signal output (3d output).
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
endChain_test = 0,0,0 : pm.endChain(pm.basicBlock);

symbols.name: openString
symbols.summary: A bidirectional block implementing a basic "generic" string with a selectable excitation position. Lowpass filters are built-in and allow to simulate the effect of dispersion on the sound and thus to change the "stiffness" of the string.

params.name: stiffness
params.description: the stiffness of the string (0-1) (1 for max stiffness)

params.name: pluckPosition
params.description: excitation position (0-1) (1 is bottom)

params.name: excitation
params.description: the excitation signal

symbols.testCode:

symbols.name: elecGuitarBridge
symbols.summary: Bidirectional block implementing a simple electric guitar bridge. This block is based on [`bridgeFilter`](#bridgeFilter). The bridge doesn't implement transmittance since it is not meant to be connected to a body (unlike acoustic guitar). It also partially sets the resonance duration of the string with the nuts used on the other side.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
elecGuitarBridge_test = 0,0,0 : pm.elecGuitarBridge;

symbols.name: elecGuitarNuts
symbols.summary: Bidirectional block implementing a simple electric guitar nuts. This block is based on [`bridgeFilter`](#bridgeFilter) and does essentially the same thing as [`elecGuitarBridge`](#elecguitarbridge), but on the other side of the chain. It also partially sets the resonance duration of the string with the bridge used on the other side.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
elecGuitarNuts_test = 0,0,0 : pm.elecGuitarNuts;

symbols.name: guitarBridge
symbols.summary: Bidirectional block implementing a simple acoustic guitar bridge. This bridge damps more hight frequencies than [`elecGuitarBridge`](#elecguitarbridge) and implements a transmittance filter. It also partially sets the resonance duration of the string with the nuts used on the other side.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
guitarBridge_test = 0,0,0 : pm.guitarBridge;

symbols.name: guitarNuts
symbols.summary: Bidirectional block implementing a simple acoustic guitar nuts. This nuts damps more hight frequencies than [`elecGuitarNuts`](#elecguitarnuts) and implements a transmittance filter. It also partially sets the resonance duration of the string with the bridge used on the other side.
symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
guitarNuts_test = 0,0,0 : pm.guitarNuts;

symbols.name: modeInterpRes
symbols.summary: Modular string instrument resonator based on IR measurements made on 3D printed models. The 2D space allowing for the control of the shape and the scale of the model is enabled by interpolating between modes parameters. More information about this technique/project can be found here: <https://ccrma.stanford.edu/~rmichon/3dPrintingModeling/>.

params.name: nModes
params.description: number of modeled modes (40 max)

params.name: x
params.description: shape of the resonator (0: square, 1: square with rounded corners, 2: round)

params.name: y
params.description: scale of the resonator (0: small, 1: medium, 2: large)

symbols.name: marimbaBarModel
symbols.summary: Generic marimba tone bar modal model. This model was generated using `mesh2faust` from a 3D CAD model of a marimba tone bar (`libraries/modalmodels/marimbaBar`). The corresponding CAD model is that of a C2 tone bar (original fundamental frequency: ~65Hz). While `marimbaBarModel` allows to translate the harmonic content of the generated sound by providing a frequency (`freq`), mode transposition has limits and the model will sound less and less like a marimba tone bar as it diverges from C2. To make an accurate model of a marimba, we'd want to have an independent model for each bar... This model contains 5 excitation positions going linearly from the center bottom to the center top of the bar. Obviously, a model with more excitation position could be regenerated using `mesh2faust`.

params.name: excitation
params.description: excitation signal

params.name: freq
params.description: fundamental frequency of the bar

params.name: exPos
params.description: excitation position (0-4)

params.name: t60
params.description: T60 in seconds (recommended value: 0.1)

params.name: t60DecayRatio
params.description: T60 decay ratio (recommended value: 1)

params.name: t60DecaySlope
params.description: T60 decay slope (recommended value: 5)

symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
marimbaBarModel_test = pm.marimbaBarModel(220);

params.name: tubeLength

params.name: excitation

symbols.summary: Simple marimba physical model implementing a single tone bar connected to tube. This model is scalable and can be adapted to any size of bar/tube (see [`marimbaBarModel`](#marimbabarmodel) to know more about the limitations of this type of system). This function also implement a virtual exciter to drive the model.

params.description: gain of the strike (0-1)

symbols.name: russianBellModel
symbols.summary: Russian church bell modal model generated by `mesh2faust` from `libraries/modalmodels/russianBell`. Modeled after D.Bartocha and Baron, Influence of Tin Bronze Melting and Pouring Parameters on Its Properties and Bell' Tone, Archives of Foundry Engineering, 2016. Model height is 2 m. This model contains 7 excitation positions going linearly from the bottom to the top of the bell. Obviously, a model with more excitation position could be regenerated using `mesh2faust`.

symbols.name: formantFilterFofCycle
symbols.summary: Formant filter based on a single FOF filter. Formant parameters are linearly interpolated allowing to go smoothly from one vowel to another. A cycle of `n` fof filters with sample-and-hold is used so that the fof filter parameters can be varied in realtime. This technique is more robust but more computationally expensive than [`formantFilterFofSmooth`](#formantFilterFofSmooth).Voice type can be selected but must correspond to the frequency range of the provided source to be realistic.

params.description: the voice type (0: alto, 1: bass, 2: countertenor,

params.description: number of formant regions in frequency domain, typically 5

params.name: i
params.description: formant number (i.e. 0 - 4) used to index formant data value arrays

params.name: freq
params.description: fundamental frequency of excitation signal. Used to calculate

Voice type can be selected but must correspond to the frequency range of the provided source to be realistic.

symbols.name: SFFormantModel
symbols.summary: Simple formant/vocal synthesizer based on a source/filter model. The `source` and `filterbank` must be specified by the user. `filterbank` must take the same input parameters as [`formantFilterbank`](#formantFilterbank) (`BP`/`FofCycle` /`FofSmooth`). Formant parameters are linearly interpolated allowing to go smoothly from one vowel to another. Voice type can be selected but must correspond to the frequency range of the synthesized voice to be realistic.

params.name: voiceType
params.description: the voice type (0: alto, 1: bass, 2: countertenor, 3: soprano, 4: tenor)

params.name: vowel
params.description: the vowel (0: a, 1: e, 2: i, 3: o, 4: u

params.name: exType
params.description: voice vs. fricative sound ratio (0-1 where 1 is 100% fricative)

params.name: freq
params.description: the fundamental frequency of the source signal

params.name: gain
params.description: linear gain multiplier to multiply the source by

params.name: isFof
params.description: whether model is FOF based (0: no, 1: yes)

symbols.testCode:
pm = library("physmodels.lib");
SFFormantModel_test = pm.SFFormantModel(0, 0, 0.5, 0.6, 100, 2, 1, 1);

symbols.name: SFFormantModelFofCycle
symbols.summary: Simple formant/vocal synthesizer based on a source/filter model. The source is just a periodic impulse and the "filter" is a bank of FOF filters. Formant parameters are linearly interpolated allowing to go smoothly from one vowel to another. Voice type can be selected but must correspond to the frequency range of the synthesized voice to be realistic. This model does not work with noise in the source signal so exType has been removed and model does not depend on SFFormantModel function.

params.name: voiceType
params.description: the voice type (0: alto, 1: bass, 2: countertenor, 3: soprano, 4: tenor)

params.name: vowel
params.description: the vowel (0: a, 1: e, 2: i, 3: o, 4: u

params.name: freq
params.description: the fundamental frequency of the source signal

params.name: gain
params.description: linear gain multiplier to multiply the source by

symbols.testCode:

symbols.name: parReduce
symbols.summary: `parReduce(op,N)` combines a set of `N` parallel signals into a single one using a binary operation `op`. With `parReduce`, this reduction process simultaneously occurs on each half of the incoming signals. In other words, `parReduce(max,256)` is equivalent to `parReduce(max,128),parReduce(max,128) : max`. To be used with `parReduce`, binary operation `op` must be associative. Additionally, the concept of a binary operation extends to operations that have `2*n` inputs and `n` outputs. For example, complex signals can be simulated using two signals for the real and imaginary parts. In such case, a binary operation would have 4 inputs and 2 outputs. Please note also that `parReduce` is faster than `topReduce` or `botReduce` for large number of signals. It is therefore the recommended operation whenever `op` is associative.

params.name: op
params.description: is a binary operation

params.name: N
params.description: is the number of incomming signals (`N>0`). We use a capital letter

symbols.testCode:
rm = library("reducemaps.lib");
parReduce_test = (1,2,3,4) : rm.parReduce(+, 4);

symbols.name: topReduce
symbols.summary: `topReduce(op,N)` involves combining a set of `N` parallel signals into a single one using a binary operation `op`. With `topReduce`, the reduction process starts from the top two incoming signals, down to the bottom. In other words, `topReduce(max,256)` is equivalent to `topReduce(max,255),_ : max`. Contrary to `parReduce`, the binary operation `op` doesn't have to be associative here. Like with `parReduce` the concept of a binary operation can be extended to operations that have 2*n inputs and n outputs. For example, complex signals can be simulated using two signals representing the real and imaginary parts. In such cases, a binary operation would have 4 inputs and 2 outputs.

params.name: op
params.description: is a binary operation

params.name: N
params.description: is the number of incomming signals (`N>0`). We use a capital letter

symbols.testCode:
rm = library("reducemaps.lib");
topReduce_test = (1,2,3,4) : rm.topReduce(+, 4);

symbols.name: botReduce
symbols.summary: `botReduce(op,N)` combines a set of `N` parallel signals into a single one using a binary operation `op`. With `botReduce`, the reduction process starts from the bottom two incoming signals, up to the top. In other words, `botReduce(max,256)` is equivalent to `_,botReduce(max,255): max`. Contrary to `parReduce`, the binary operation `op` doesn't have to be associative here. Like with `parReduce` the concept of a binary operation can be extended to operations that have 2*n inputs and n outputs. For example, complex signals can be simulated using two signals representing the real and imaginary parts. In such cases, a binary operation would have 4 inputs and 2 outputs.
symbols.testCode:
rm = library("reducemaps.lib");
botReduce_test = (1,2,3,4) : rm.botReduce(+, 4);

symbols.name: reduce
symbols.summary: Reduce a block of `n` consecutive samples of the incomming signal using a binary operation `op`. For example: `reduce(max,128)` will compute the maximun value of each block of 128 samples. Please note that the resulting value, while computed continuously, will be constant for the duration of a block. A new value is only produced at the end of a block. Note also that blocks should be of at least one sample (n>0).

params.name: op
params.description: is a binary operation

params.name: n
params.description: is the number of consecutive samples in a block.

symbols.testCode:
rm = library("reducemaps.lib");
reduce_test = rm.reduce(max, 4, hslider("reduce:input", 0, -1, 1, 0.01));

symbols.name: reducemap
symbols.summary: Like `reduce` but a `foo` function is applied to the result. From a mathematical point of view: `reducemap(op,foo,n)` is equivalent to `reduce(op,n):foo` but more efficient.

params.name: op
params.description: is a binary operation

params.name: foo
params.description: is a function applied to the result of the reduction

params.name: n
params.description: is the number of consecutive samples in a block.

symbols.testCode:
rm = library("reducemaps.lib");
reducemap_test = rm.reducemap(+, /(4), 4, hslider("reducemap:input", 0, -1, 1, 0.01));

symbols.name: jcrev
symbols.summary: This artificial reverberator take a mono signal and output stereo (`satrev`) and quad (`jcrev`). They were implemented by John Chowning in the MUS10 computer-music language (descended from Music V by Max Mathews).  They are Schroeder Reverberators, well tuned for their size. Nowadays, the more expensive freeverb is more commonly used (see the Faust examples directory). `jcrev` reverb below was made from a listing of "RV", dated April 14, 1972, which was recovered from an old SAIL DART backup tape. John Chowning thinks this might be the one that became the well known and often copied JCREV. `jcrev` is a standard Faust function.

symbols.name: satrev
symbols.summary: This artificial reverberator take a mono signal and output stereo (`satrev`) and quad (`jcrev`).  They were implemented by John Chowning in the MUS10 computer-music language (descended from Music V by Max Mathews).  They are Schroeder Reverberators, well tuned for their size. Nowadays, the more expensive freeverb is more commonly used (see the Faust examples directory). `satrev` was made from a listing of "SATREV", dated May 15, 1971, which was recovered from an old SAIL DART backup tape. John Chowning thinks this might be the one used on his often-heard brass canon sound examples, one of which can be found at <https://ccrma.stanford.edu/~jos/wav/FM-BrassCanon2.wav>.

symbols.name: fdnrev0

params.name: f2
params.description: frequency (Hz) above f1 where T60 = t60m/2 (see below)

symbols.name: mono_freeverb
symbols.summary: A simple Schroeder reverberator primarily developed by "Jezar at Dreampoint" that is extensively used in the free-software world. It uses four Schroeder allpasses in series and eight parallel Schroeder-Moorer filtered-feedback comb-filters for each audio channel, and is said to be especially well tuned. `mono_freeverb` is a standard Faust function.

params.name: fb2
params.description: coefficient of the allpass comb filters (0-1)

params.name: damp

params.name: spread
params.description: spatial spread in number of samples (for stereo)

symbols.name: jpverb
symbols.summary: An algorithmic reverb (stereo in/out), inspired by the lush chorused sound of certain vintage Lexicon and Alesis reverberation units. Designed to sound great with synthetic sound sources, rather than sound like a realistic space.
symbols.usage: _,_ : jpverb(t60, damp, size, early_diff, mod_depth, mod_freq, low, mid, high, low_cutoff, high_cutoff) : _,_

params.name: t60
params.description: approximate reverberation time in seconds ([0.1..60] sec) (T60 - the time for the reverb to decay by 60db when damp == 0 ). Does not effect early reflections

params.name: damp
params.description: controls damping of high-frequencies as the reverb decays. 0 is no damping, 1 is very strong damping. Values should be in the range ([0..1])

params.name: size
params.description: scales size of delay-lines within the reverberator, producing the impression of a larger or smaller space. Values below 1 can sound metallic. Values should be in the range [0.5..5]

params.name: early_diff
params.description: controls shape of early reflections. Values of 0.707 or more produce smooth exponential decay. Lower values produce a slower build-up of echoes. Values should be in the range ([0..1])

params.name: mod_depth
params.description: depth ([0..1]) of delay-line modulation. Use in combination with `mod_freq` to set amount of chorusing within the structure

params.name: mod_freq
params.description: frequency ([0..10] Hz) of delay-line modulation. Use in combination with `mod_depth` to set amount of chorusing within the structure

params.name: low
params.description: multiplier ([0..1]) for the reverberation time within the low band

params.name: mid
params.description: multiplier ([0..1]) for the reverberation time within the mid band

params.name: high
params.description: multiplier ([0..1]) for the reverberation time within the high band

params.name: low_cutoff
params.description: frequency (100..6000 Hz) at which the crossover between the low and mid bands of the reverb occurs

params.name: high_cutoff
params.description: frequency (1000..10000 Hz) at which the crossover between the mid and high bands of the reverb occurs

io.raw: _,_ : jpverb(t60, damp, size, early_diff, mod_depth, mod_freq, low, mid, high, low_cutoff, high_cutoff) : _,_

symbols.name: greyhole
symbols.summary: A complex echo-like effect (stereo in/out), inspired by the classic Eventide effect of a similar name. The effect consists of a diffuser (like a mini-reverb, structurally similar to the one used in `jpverb`) connected in a feedback system with a long, modulated delay-line. Excels at producing spacey washes of sound.
symbols.usage: _,_ : greyhole(dt, damp, size, early_diff, feedback, mod_depth, mod_freq) : _,_

params.name: dt
params.description: approximate reverberation time in seconds ([0.1..60 sec])

params.name: damp
params.description: controls damping of high-frequencies as the reverb decays. 0 is no damping, 1 is very strong damping. Values should be between ([0..1])

params.name: size
params.description: control of relative "room size" roughly in the range ([0.5..3])

params.name: early_diff
params.description: controls pattern of echoes produced by the diffuser. At very low values, the diffuser acts like a delay-line whose length is controlled by the 'size' parameter. Medium values produce a slow build-up of echoes, giving the sound a reversed-like quality. Values of 0.707 or greater than produce smooth exponentially decaying echoes. Values should be in the range ([0..1])

params.name: feedback
params.description: amount of feedback through the system. Sets the number of repeating echoes. A setting of 1.0 produces infinite sustain. Values should be in the range ([0..1])

params.name: mod_depth
params.description: depth ([0..1]) of delay-line modulation. Use in combination with `mod_freq` to produce chorus and pitch-variations in the echoes

params.name: mod_freq
params.description: frequency ([0..10] Hz) of delay-line modulation. Use in combination with `mod_depth` to produce chorus and pitch-variations in the echoes

io.raw: _,_ : greyhole(dt, damp, size, early_diff, feedback, mod_depth, mod_freq) : _,_

symbols.name: kb_rom_rev1
symbols.summary: Reverberator based on Keith Barr's all-pass single feedback loop reverb topology. Originally designed for the Spin Semiconductor FV-1 chip, this code is an adaptation of the rom_rev1.spn file, part of the Spin Semiconductor Free DSP Programs available on the Spin Semiconductor website. It was submitted by Keith Barr himself and written in Spin Semiconductor Assembly, a dedicated assembly language for programming the FV-1 chip. In this topology, when multiple delays and all-pass filters are placed in a loop, sound injected into the loop will recirculate, increasing the density of any impulse as the signal successively passes through the all-pass filters. The result, after a short period of time, is a wash of sound, completely diffused into a natural reverb tail. The reverb typically has a mono input (as from a single source) but benefits from a stereo output, providing the listener with a fuller, more immersive reverberant image.

params.name: rt
params.description: coefficent of the decay of the reverb (0-1)

params.name: damp
params.description: coefficient of the lowpass filters (0-1)

symbols.name: springreverb
symbols.summary: Mono spring-inspired reverb originally designed for the Chaos Audio Stratus, which defines all parameters in the [0..10] range. They have been remapped to more typical [0..1] ranges in this implementation. Uses a diffusion stage into a bank of damped delay lines with Hadamard feedback mixing to emulate the lively, metallic character of multi-spring tanks.

params.description: feedback amount controlling decay length ([0..1])

params.name: blend

params.name: tone
params.description: lowpass cutoff applied to the wet path ([0..1])

params.name: tension
params.description: base spring delay time and tail length ([0..1])

params.name: springs
params.description: spacing preset between spring delays (0 = left, 1 = right, 2 = middle)

symbols.summary: Interleave R x C cables from column order to row order. That is, transpose the input CxR matrix, the first R inputs is the first row. input : `x(0), x(1), x(2) ..., x(row*col-1)` output: `x(0+0*row), x(0+1*row), x(0+2*row), ..., x(1+0*row), x(1+1*row), x(1+2*row), ...`

symbols.name: bubbleSort
symbols.summary: Sort a set of N parallel signals in ascending order on-the-fly through the Bubble Sort algorithm. Mechanism: having a set of N parallel signals indexed from 0 to N - 1, compare the first pair of signals and swap them if sig[0] > sig[1]; repeat the pair comparison for the signals sig[1] and sig[2], then again recursively until reaching the signals sig[N - 2] and sig[N - 1]; by the end, the largest element in the set will be placed last; repeat the process for the remaining N - 1 signals until there is a single pair left. Note that this implementation will always perform the worst-case computation, O(n^2). Even though the Bubble Sort algorithm is one of the least efficient ones, it is a useful example of how automatic sorting can be implemented at the signal level.

params.name: N
params.description: the number of signals to be sorted (must be an int >= 0, as a constant numerical expression)

A pan of 0 preserves the left channel and silences the right channel. A pan of 1 has the opposite effect. A pan value of 0.5 applies a gain of 0.5 to both channels.

symbols.name: vecOp
symbols.summary: This function is a generalisation of Faust's iterators such as `prod` and `sum`, and it allows to perform operations on an arbitrary number of vectors, provided that they all have the same length. Unlike Faust's iterators `prod` and `sum` where the vector size is equal to one and the vector space dimension must be specified by the user, this function will infer the vector space dimension and vector size based on the vectors list that we provide. The outputs of the function are equal to the vector size, whereas the number of inputs is dependent on whether the elements of the vectors provided expect an incoming signal themselves or not. We will see a clarifying example later; in general, the number of total inputs will be the sum of the inputs in each input vector. Note that we must provide a list of at least two vectors, each with a size that is greater or equal to one.

symbols.name: bpar
symbols.summary: Balanced `par` where the repeated expression doesn't depend on a variable. The built-in `par` is implemented as an unbalanced tree, and also has to substitute the variable into the repeated expression, which is expensive even when the variable doesn't appear. This version is implemented as a balanced tree (which allows node reuse during tree traversal) and also doesn't search for the variable. This can be much faster than `par` to compile.
symbols.usage: si.bus(N * inputs(f)) : bpar(N, f) : si.bus(N * outputs(f))

params.name: N
params.description: number of repetitions, minimum 1, a constant numerical expression

params.name: f
params.description: an arbitrary expression

io.raw: si.bus(N * inputs(f)) : bpar(N, f) : si.bus(N * outputs(f))

symbols.testCode:
// square each of 4000 inputs
process = si.bpar(4000, (_ <: _, _ : *));

symbols.name: moog_vcf
symbols.summary: Moog "Voltage Controlled Filter" (VCF) in "analog" form. Moog VCF implemented using the same logical block diagram as the classic analog circuit.  As such, it neglects the one-sample delay associated with the feedback path around the four one-poles. This extra delay alters the response, especially at high frequencies (see reference [1] for details). See `moog_vcf_2b` below for a more accurate implementation.

params.name: res
params.description: normalized amount of corner-resonance between 0 and 1

params.name: fr
params.description: corner-resonance frequency in Hz (less than SR/6.3 or so)

symbols.name: moog_vcf_2b[n]
symbols.summary: Moog "Voltage Controlled Filter" (VCF) as two biquads. Implementation of the ideal Moog VCF transfer function factored into second-order sections. As a result, it is more accurate than `moog_vcf` above, but its coefficient formulas are more complex when one or both parameters are varied.  Here, res is the fourth root of that in `moog_vcf`, so, as the sampling rate approaches infinity, `moog_vcf(res,fr)` becomes equivalent to `moog_vcf_2b[n](res^4,fr)` (when res and fr are constant). `moog_vcf_2b` uses two direct-form biquads (`tf2`). `moog_vcf_2bn` uses two protected normalized-ladder biquads (`tf2np`).

params.name: res
params.description: normalized amount of corner-resonance between 0 and 1

params.name: fr
params.description: corner-resonance frequency in Hz

symbols.name: moogLadder
symbols.summary: Virtual analog model of the 4th-order Moog Ladder (without any nonlinearities), which is arguably the most well-known ladder filter in analog synthesizers. Several 1st-order filters are cascaded in series. Feedback is then used, in part, to control the cut-off frequency and the resonance.

params.name: normFreq
params.description: normalized frequency (0-1)

params.name: Q
params.description: quality factor between .707 (0 feedback coefficient) to 25 (feedback = 4, which is the self-oscillating threshold).

symbols.name: lowpassLadder4
symbols.summary: Topology-preserving transform implementation of a four-pole ladder lowpass. This is essentially the same filter as the moogLadder above except for the parameters, which will be expressed in Hz, for the cutoff, and as a raw feedback coefficient, for the resonance. Also, note that the parameter order has changed.

params.name: k
params.description: feedback coefficient between 0 and 4, which is the stability threshold.

params.name: CF
params.description: the filter's cutoff in Hz.

symbols.name: moogHalfLadder
symbols.summary: Virtual analog model of the 2nd-order Moog Half Ladder (simplified version of `(ve.)moogLadder`). Several 1st-order filters are cascaded in series. Feedback is then used, in part, to control the cut-off frequency and the resonance. This filter was implemented in Faust by Eric Tarr during the [2019 Embedded DSP With Faust Workshop](https://ccrma.stanford.edu/workshops/faust-embedded-19/).

params.name: normFreq
params.description: normalized frequency (0-1)

params.name: Q
params.description: q

symbols.name: diodeLadder
symbols.summary: 4th order virtual analog diode ladder filter. In addition to the individual states used within each independent 1st-order filter, there are also additional feedback paths found in the block diagram. These feedback paths are labeled as connecting states. Rather than separately storing these connecting states in the Faust implementation, they are simply implicitly calculated by tracing back to the other states (`s1`,`s2`,`s3`,`s4`) each recursive step. This filter was implemented in Faust by Eric Tarr during the [2019 Embedded DSP With Faust Workshop](https://ccrma.stanford.edu/workshops/faust-embedded-19/).

params.name: normFreq
params.description: normalized frequency (0-1)

params.name: Q
params.description: q

symbols.name: sallenKeyOnePole
symbols.summary: Sallen-Key generic One Pole filter that produces the LPF and HPF outputs (see description above). For the Faust implementation of this filter, recursion (`letrec`) is used for storing filter "states". The output (e.g. `y`) is calculated by using the input signal and the previous states of the filter. During the current recursive step, the states of the filter (e.g. `s`) for the next step are also calculated. Admittedly, this is not an efficient way to implement a filter because it requires independently calculating the output and each state during each recursive step. However, it works as a way to store and use "states" within the constraints of Faust. The simplest example is the 1st-order LPF (shown on the cover of Zavalishin 2018 and Fig 4.3 of <https://www.willpirkle.com/706-2/>). Here, the input signal is split in parallel for the calculation of the output signal, `y`, and the state `s`. The value of the state is only used for feedback to the next step of recursion. It is blocked (!) from also being routed to the output. A trick used for calculating the state `s` is to observe that the input to the delay block is the sum of two signal: what appears to be a feedforward path and a feedback path. In reality, the signals being summed are identical `(signal*2)` plus the value of the current state.

params.name: normFreq
params.description: normalized frequency (0-1)

symbols.name: sallenKeyOnePoleLPF
symbols.summary: Sallen-Key One Pole lowpass filter (see description above). Specialize the generic implementation: keep the first LPF output, the compiler will only generate the needed code.

params.name: normFreq

symbols.name: sallenKeyOnePoleHPF
symbols.summary: Sallen-Key One Pole Highpass filter (see description above). The dry input signal is routed in parallel to the output. The LPF'd signal is subtracted from the input so that the HPF remains. Specialize the generic implementation: keep the second HPF output, the compiler will only generate the needed code.

params.name: normFreq
params.description: normalized frequency (0-1)

symbols.name: sallenKey2ndOrder
symbols.summary: Sallen-Key generic 2nd order filter that produces the LPF, BPF and HPF outputs. This is a 2nd-order Sallen-Key state-variable filter. The idea is that by "tapping" into different points in the circuit, different filters (LPF,BPF,HPF) can be achieved. See Figure 4.6 of <https://www.willpirkle.com/706-2/> This is also a good example of the next step for generalizing the Faust programming approach used for all these VA filters. In this case, there are three things to calculate each recursive step (`y`,`s1`,`s2`). For each thing, the circuit is only calculated up to that point. Comparing the LPF to BPF, the output signal (`y`) is calculated similarly. Except, the output of the BPF stops earlier in the circuit. Similarly, the states (`s1` and `s2`) only differ in that `s2` includes a couple more terms beyond what is used for `s1`.

params.name: normFreq
params.description: normalized frequency (0-1)

params.name: Q
params.description: quality factor controlling the sharpness/resonance of the filter around the center frequency (CF). For bandpass filters, higher Q increases the gain at the center frequency. Must be in the range `[ma.EPSILON, ma.MAX]`

symbols.summary: Resistor + current Out. A basic adaptor implementing a resistor for use within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. The resistor will also pass the current through itself as an output of the model.

params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: R
params.description: Resistance/Impedance of the resistor being modeled in Ohms.

symbols.name: u_voltage
symbols.summary: Unadapted Ideal Voltage Source. An adaptor implementing an ideal voltage source within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as the root/top element of the connection tree. Can be used for either DC (constant) or AC (signal) voltage sources.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: ein
params.description: Voltage/Potential across ideal voltage source in Volts

symbols.name: u_current
symbols.summary: Unadapted Ideal Current Source. An unadapted adaptor implementing an ideal current source within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as the root/top element of the connection tree. Can be used for either DC (constant) or AC (signal) current sources.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: jin
params.description: Current through the ideal current source in Amps

symbols.name: resVoltage
symbols.summary: Adapted Resistive Voltage Source. An adaptor implementing a resistive voltage source within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. It is comprised of an ideal voltage source in series with a resistor. Can be used for either DC (constant) or AC (signal) voltage sources.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: R
params.description: Resistance/Impedance of the series resistor in Ohms

params.name: ein
params.description: Voltage/Potential of the ideal voltage source in Volts

symbols.name: resVoltage_Vout
symbols.summary: Adapted Resistive Voltage Source + voltage output. An adaptor implementing an adapted resistive voltage source within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. It is comprised of an ideal voltage source in series with a resistor. Can be used for either DC (constant) or AC (signal) voltage sources. The resistive voltage source will also pass the voltage across it as an output of the model.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: R
params.description: Resistance/Impedance of the series resistor in Ohms

params.name: ein
params.description: Voltage/Potential across ideal voltage source in Volts

symbols.name: u_resVoltage
symbols.summary: Unadapted Resistive Voltage Source. An unadapted adaptor implementing a resistive voltage source within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as the root/top element of the connection tree. It is comprised of an ideal voltage source in series with a resistor. Can be used for either DC (constant) or AC (signal) voltage sources.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: R
params.description: Resistance/Impedance of the series resistor in Ohms

params.name: ein
params.description: Voltage/Potential across ideal voltage source in Volts

symbols.name: resCurrent
symbols.summary: Adapted Resistive Current Source. An adaptor implementing a resistive current source within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. It is comprised of an ideal current source in parallel with a resistor. Can be used for either DC (constant) or AC (signal) current sources.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: R
params.description: Resistance/Impedance of the parallel resistor in Ohms

params.name: jin
params.description: Current through the ideal current source in Amps

symbols.name: u_resCurrent
symbols.summary: Unadapted Resistive Current Source. An unadapted adaptor implementing a resistive current source within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as the root/top element of the connection tree. It is comprised of an ideal current source in parallel with a resistor. Can be used for either DC (constant) or AC (signal) current sources.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: R
params.description: Resistance/Impedance of the series resistor in Ohms

params.name: jin
params.description: Current through the ideal current source in Amps

symbols.name: u_switch
symbols.summary: Unadapted Ideal Switch. An unadapted adaptor implementing an ideal switch for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as the root/top element of the connection tree

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: lambda
params.description: switch state control. -1 for closed switch, 1 for open switch.

symbols.name: capacitor
symbols.summary: Adapted Capacitor. A basic adaptor implementing a capacitor for use within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. This capacitor model was digitized using the bi-linear transform.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: R
params.description: Capacitance/Impedance of the capacitor being modeled in Farads.

symbols.name: capacitor_Vout
symbols.summary: Adapted Capacitor + voltage out. A basic adaptor implementing a capacitor for use within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. The capacitor will also pass the voltage across itself as an output of the model. This capacitor model was digitized using the bi-linear transform.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: R
params.description: Capacitance/Impedence of the capacitor being modeled in Farads

symbols.name: capacitor_Iout
symbols.summary: Adapted Capacitor + current out. A basic adaptor implementing a capacitor for use within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. The capacitor will also pass the current through itself as an output of the model. This capacitor model was digitized using the bi-linear transform.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: C
params.description: Capacitance of the capacitor being modeled in Farads.

symbols.name: inductor
symbols.summary: Unadapted Inductor. A basic adaptor implementing an inductor for use within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. This inductor model was digitized using the bi-linear transform.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: R
params.description: Inductance/Impedance of the inductor being modeled in Henries

symbols.name: inductor_Vout
symbols.summary: Unadapted Inductor + Voltage out. A basic adaptor implementing an inductor for use within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. The inductor will also pass the voltage across itself as an output of the model. This inductor model was digitized using the bi-linear transform.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: R
params.description: Inductance/Impedance of the inductor being modeled in Henries

symbols.name: inductor_Iout
symbols.summary: Unadapted Inductor + current out. A basic adaptor implementing an inductor for use within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as a leaf/terminating element of the connection tree. The inductor will also pass the current through itself as an output of the model. This inductor model was digitized using the bi-linear transform.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared.

params.name: L
params.description: Inductance of the inductor being modeled in Henries.

symbols.name: u_idealDiode
symbols.summary: Unadapted Ideal Diode. An unadapted adaptor implementing an ideal diode for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as the root/top element of the connection tree.

symbols.name: u_chua
symbols.summary: Unadapted Chua Diode. An adaptor implementing the chua diode / non-linear resistor within Wave Digital Filter connection trees. It should be used as the root/top element of the connection tree.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: G1
params.description: resistance parameter 1 of the chua diode

params.name: G2
params.description: resistance parameter 2 of the chua diode

params.name: V0
params.description: voltage parameter of the chua diode

symbols.name: lambert
symbols.summary: An implementation of the lambert function. It uses Halley's method of iteration to approximate the output. Included in the WD library for use in non-linear diode models. Adapted from K M Brigg's c++ lambert function approximation.

params.name: n
params.description: value at which the lambert function will be evaluated

params.name: itr
params.description: number of iterations before output

symbols.name: u_diodePair
symbols.summary: Unadapted pair of diodes facing in opposite directions. An unadapted adaptor implementing two antiparallel diodes for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. The behavior is approximated using Schottkey's ideal diode law.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: Is
params.description: saturation current of the diodes

params.name: Vt
params.description: thermal resistances of the diodes

symbols.testCode:
wd = library("wdmodels.lib");

u_diodePair_test = wd.u_diodePair(2, 1e-12, 0.025);

symbols.name: u_diodeSingle
symbols.summary: Unadapted single diode. An unadapted adaptor implementing a single diode for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. The behavior is approximated using Schottkey's ideal diode law.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: Is
params.description: saturation current of the diodes

params.name: Vt
params.description: thermal resistances of the diodes

symbols.testCode:
wd = library("wdmodels.lib");

u_diodeSingle_test = wd.u_diodeSingle(2, 8e-13, 0.026);

symbols.name: u_diodeAntiparallel
symbols.summary: Unadapted set of antiparallel diodes with M diodes facing forwards and N diodes facing backwards. An unadapted adaptor implementing antiparallel diodes for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. The behavior is approximated using Schottkey's ideal diode law.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: Is
params.description: saturation current of the diodes

params.name: Vt
params.description: thermal resistances of the diodes

symbols.testCode:
wd = library("wdmodels.lib");
u_diodeAntiparallel_test = wd.u_diodeAntiparallel(2, 1e-12, 0.025, 2, 2);

symbols.name: omega
symbols.summary: Wright Omega function approximation for diode modeling. An attempt at the Wright Omega function using a piecewise approach with third‑order polynomials and Newton‑Raphson refinement. This matches the approach used in chowdsp::wdf for diode modeling.

params.name: x
params.description: input value

symbols.testCode:
wd = library("wdmodels.lib");
omega_test = wd.omega(0.5);

symbols.name: u_diodeAntiparallel_omega
symbols.summary: Unadapted antiparallel diodes using Wright Omega function. An unadapted adaptor implementing antiparallel diodes for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. Uses the Wright Omega function instead of Lambert W.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: Is
params.description: saturation current of the diodes (e.g., 2.52e-9 for 1N4148)

params.name: Vt
params.description: thermal voltage (typically 0.02585 at room temperature)

symbols.testCode:
wd = library("wdmodels.lib");
u_diodeAntiparallel_omega_test = wd.u_diodeAntiparallel_omega(2, 2.52e-9, 0.02585, 1, 1);

symbols.name: u_parallel2Port
symbols.summary: Unadapted 2-port parallel connection. An unadapted adaptor implementing a 2-port parallel connection between adaptors for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. Elements connected to this adaptor will behave as if connected in parallel in circuit.

symbols.name: parallel2Port
symbols.summary: Adapted 2-port parallel connection. An adaptor implementing a 2-port parallel connection between adaptors for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. Elements connected to this adaptor will behave as if connected in parallel in circuit.

symbols.name: u_series2Port
symbols.summary: Unadapted 2-port series connection. An unadapted adaptor implementing a 2-port series connection between adaptors for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. Elements connected to this adaptor will behave as if connected in series in circuit.

symbols.name: series2Port
symbols.summary: Adapted 2-port series connection. An adaptor implementing a 2-port series connection between adaptors for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. Elements connected to this adaptor will behave as if connected in series in circuit.

symbols.name: parallelCurrent
symbols.summary: Adapted 2-port parallel connection + ideal current source. An adaptor implementing a 2-port series connection and internal idealized current source between adaptors for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. This adaptor connects the two connected elements and an additional ideal current source in parallel.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: jin
params.description: Current through the ideal current source in Amps

symbols.name: seriesVoltage
symbols.summary: Adapted 2-port series connection + ideal voltage source. An adaptor implementing a 2-port series connection and internal ideal voltage source between adaptors for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. This adaptor connects the two connected adaptors and an additional ideal voltage source in series.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: vin
params.description: voltage across the ideal current source in Volts

symbols.name: u_transformer
symbols.summary: Unadapted ideal transformer. An adaptor implementing an ideal transformer for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. The first downward-facing port corresponds to the primary winding connections, and the second downward-facing port to the secondary winding connections.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: tr
params.description: the turn ratio between the windings on the primary and secondary coils

symbols.name: transformer
symbols.summary: Adapted ideal transformer. An adaptor implementing an ideal transformer for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. The upward-facing port corresponds to the primary winding connections, and the downward-facing port to the secondary winding connections

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: tr
params.description: the turn ratio between the windings on the primary and secondary coils

symbols.name: u_transformerActive
symbols.summary: Unadapted ideal active transformer. An adaptor implementing an ideal transformer for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. The first downward-facing port corresponds to the primary winding connections, and the second downward-facing port to the secondary winding connections.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: gamma1
params.description: the turn ratio describing the voltage relationship between the primary and secondary coils

params.name: gamma2
params.description: the turn ratio describing the current relationship between the primary and secondary coils

symbols.name: transformerActive
symbols.summary: Adapted ideal active transformer. An adaptor implementing an ideal active transformer for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. The upward-facing port corresponds to the primary winding connections, and the downward-facing port to the secondary winding connections

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: gamma1
params.description: the turn ratio describing the voltage relationship between the primary and secondary coils

params.name: gamma2
params.description: the turn ratio describing the current relationship between the primary and secondary coils

symbols.name: parallel
symbols.summary: Adapted 3-port parallel connection. An adaptor implementing a 3-port parallel connection between adaptors for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. This adaptor is used to connect adaptors simulating components connected in parallel in the circuit.

symbols.name: series
symbols.summary: Adapted 3-port series connection. An adaptor implementing a 3-port series connection between adaptors for Wave Digital Filter connection trees. This adaptor is used to connect adaptors simulating components connected in series in the circuit.

symbols.name: u_sixportPassive
symbols.summary: Unadapted six-port rigid connection. An adaptor implementing a six-port passive rigid connection between elements. It implements the simplest possible rigid connection found in the Fender Bassman Tonestack circuit.
symbols.usage: tree = u_sixportPassive : (A, B, C, D, E, F));

io.raw: tree = u_sixportPassive : (A, B, C, D, E, F));

symbols.name: genericNode
symbols.summary: Function for generating an adapted node from another faust function or scattering matrix. This function generates a node which is suitable for use in the connection tree structure. `genericNode` separates the function that it is passed into upward-going and downward-going waves.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: scatter
params.description: the function which describes the the node's scattering behavior

params.name: upRes
params.description: the function which describes the node's upward-facing port-resistance

symbols.name: genericNode_Vout
symbols.summary: Function for generating a terminating/leaf node which gives the voltage across itself as a model output. This function generates a node which is suitable for use in the connection tree structure. It also calculates the voltage across the element and gives it as a model output.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: scatter
params.description: the function which describes the the node's scattering behavior

params.name: upRes
params.description: the function which describes the node's upward-facing port-resistance

symbols.name: genericNode_Iout
symbols.summary: Function for generating a terminating/leaf node which gives the current through itself as a model output. This function generates a node which is suitable for use in the connection tree structure. It also calculates the current through the element and gives it as a model output.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: scatter
params.description: the function which describes the the node's scattering behavior

params.name: upRes
params.description: the function which describes the node's upward-facing port-resistance

symbols.name: u_genericNode
symbols.summary: Function for generating an unadapted node from another Faust function or scattering matrix. This function generates a node which is suitable for use as the root of the connection tree structure.

params.name: i
params.description: index used by model-building functions. Should never be user declared

params.name: scatter
params.description: the function which describes the the node's scattering behavior

symbols.name: builddown
symbols.summary: Function for building the structure for calculating waves traveling down the WD connection tree. It recursively steps through the given tree, parametrizes the adaptors, and builds an algorithm. It is used in conjunction with the buildup() function to create a model.

symbols.name: buildup
symbols.summary: Function for building the structure for calculating waves traveling up the WD connection tree. It recursively steps through the given tree, parametrizes the adaptors, and builds an algorithm. It is used in conjunction with the builddown() function to create a full structure.

symbols.name: getres
symbols.summary: Function for determining the upward-facing port resistance of a partial WD connection tree. It recursively steps through the given tree, parametrizes the adaptors, and builds an algorithm. It is used by the buildup and builddown functions but is also helpful in testing.

symbols.name: parres
symbols.summary: Function for determining the upward-facing port resistance of a partial WD connection tree. It recursively steps through the given tree, parametrizes the adaptors, and builds an algorithm. It is used by the buildup and builddown functions but is also helpful in testing. This function is a parallelized version of `getres`.

symbols.name: buildout
symbols.summary: Function for creating the output matrix for a WD model from a WD connection tree. It recursively steps through the given tree and creates an output matrix passing only outputs.

symbols.name: buildtree
symbols.summary: Function for building the DSP model from a WD connection tree structure. It recursively steps through the given tree, parametrizes the adaptors, and builds the algorithm.
